readingAnalyses.title: Constitution of Japan (1947)
readingAnalyses.author: Government Section, SCAP (Supreme Commander for Allied Powers)
readingAnalyses.summary: The 1947 Constitution of Japan transferred sovereignty from the emperor to the people, established an extensive bill of rights, and most controversially, renounced war and the maintenance of military forces in Article 9. The Preamble declares that 'sovereign power resides with the people' and that Japan is 'resolved that never again shall we be visited with the horrors of war through the action of government.' The emperor becomes merely 'the symbol of the State and of the unity of the people' (Article 1), stripped of all political power.

keyQuotes.quote: We, the Japanese people... resolved that never again shall we be visited with the horrors of war through the action of government, do proclaim that sovereign power resides with the people.
The Emperor shall be the symbol of the State and of the unity of the people, deriving his position from the will of the people with whom resides sovereign power.

Aspiring sincerely to an international peace based on justice and order, the Japanese people forever renounce war as a sovereign right of the nation and the threat or use of force as means of settling international disputes.
Land, sea, and air forces, as well as other war potential, will never be maintained. The right of belligerency of the state will not be recognized.
Marriage shall be based only on the mutual consent of both sexes and it shall be maintained through mutual cooperation with the equal rights of husband and wife as a basis.

thematicExamples: Article 14 explicitly prohibits discrimination based on 'race, creed, sex, social status or family origin' and abolishes the peerage system - a direct challenge to Japan's hierarchical social order
Article 24 establishes marriage equality, requiring mutual consent and equal rights for both spouses - revolutionary for patriarchal Japan where women had limited rights under the Meiji Civil Code
Article 25 establishes a right to 'minimum standards of wholesome and cultured living' - a welfare state provision unusual in national constitutions
Article 20 guarantees freedom of religion and mandates separation of religion and state - ending State Shinto
The detailed criminal procedure rights (Articles 31-40) reflect American constitutional influence and respond to wartime abuses
realLifeConnections: Abe Shinzo's long campaign for constitutional revision, particularly Article 9, represents the conservative challenge to the occupation legacy - debates continue today
US demands for Japanese military burden-sharing clash with Article 9's constraints, creating ongoing tension in the alliance
Japan's Self-Defense Forces exist through creative constitutional interpretation - the 'Reverse Course' of the Cold War allowed partial rearmament
The question of whether democracy can be imposed from outside resonates with debates about Iraq and Afghanistan

readingAnalyses.title: The American School
readingAnalyses.author: Kojima Nobuo
readingAnalyses.summary: A masterpiece of postwar Japanese literature capturing the psychological complexity of occupation through a group of Japanese English teachers visiting an American dependents' school. The protagonist Isa refuses to speak English despite being an English teacher - out of pride, shame, and resistance. His foil Yamada performs exaggerated deference toward Americans while boasting of beheading POWs during the war. The story's central tension: Yamada schemes to force Isa into a humiliating 'demonstration class' in English. Throughout, the Japanese teachers confront their own abjection - walking four miles on an asphalt road made for American cars, marveling at the Americans' abundance while eating humble lunches outside the gate.

keyQuotes.quote: Isa had a firm policy of never speaking English. It was not that he could not speak it. Rather, he refused to.
It is foolish for Japanese to speak this language like foreigners. If they do, it makes them foreigners, too. And that is a real disgrace.
When you spoke it you stopped being yourself. It was too easy to be carried away by the titillation of the words, words not exactly your own.
With their money and their fancy buildings, the children can't draw worth a damn.
The only lesson we'll leave with is the one we've learned just getting here: we lost!
About twenty, I guess. Half of them must've been POWs... Naturally [Americans]... They show their lack of what you might call Oriental philosophy.

thematicExamples: Isa's refusal to speak English represents silent resistance to occupation - he would rather flee into the woods than be forced to speak
The four-mile march on asphalt road built for American cars - Japanese teachers reduced to pedestrians in their own country
The American school's glass walls and spacious grounds versus the teachers' shabby clothes and army-issue boots
Teachers turned into caricature fish in the American children's drawings - shark (Yamada), flying fish (Isa), goldfish (Michiko)
Yamada's simultaneous sycophancy toward Americans and boasting about beheading POWs reveals the psychological contradiction of occupied Japan
The Negro soldier who twice encounters Isa - first during election monitoring, then on the road - represents Isa's inability to escape the occupation
realLifeConnections: Japan-US relations today still carry echoes of this psychology: gratitude for the security alliance mixed with resentment of US military presence, especially in Okinawa
English language anxiety in Japan reflects deeper questions about national identity and relationship to the West - English remains both aspirational and threatening
The story anticipates debates about 'war responsibility' - Yamada mentions beheading POWs casually, then nervously realizes the danger of admitting it
Isa and Yamada represent two enduring strategies for dealing with dominant powers: dignified resistance vs. strategic accommodation

overallSynthesis:
These two readings together illuminate both the institutional architecture and the psychological reality of occupied Japan. The Constitution of Japan (1947) shows the formal, legal transformation: sovereignty transferred to the people, war renounced, human rights guaranteed, the emperor reduced to a symbol. Read in isolation, the constitution appears as a triumph of democratic idealism - 'the fundamental human rights by this Constitution guaranteed to the people of Japan are fruits of the age-old struggle of man to be free' (Article 97).

But 'The American School' reveals the human texture beneath these institutional changes. When Isa looks at the American schoolchildren, he sees that 'they moved through the world as if they owned it.' The Japanese teachers walk four miles on asphalt built for American cars. Their salaries are one-tenth of the American teachers'. The constitution's idealistic language about 'sovereign power residing with the people' takes on different meaning when we consider that these words were drafted by Americans in a week and presented to the Japanese as a fait accompli.

The pairing reveals the central paradox of the occupation: it was simultaneously democratizing and colonizing, liberating and humiliating. The constitution gave Japanese women legal equality; the American school made Japanese men feel small. Article 9 freed Japan from militarism; the presence of US bases reminded everyone who had won. Yamada's character embodies this contradiction perfectly - he boasts of beheading American POWs while desperately seeking American approval for a 'demonstration class.' Students should understand that 'Rebuilding Japan' was never simply a matter of new institutions, but involved the reconstruction of Japanese identity itself.
executiveSummary:
American occupation (1945-1952) transformed Japan more thoroughly than the Meiji transformation had. The new constitution - drafted largely by Americans in one week - established popular sovereignty, renounced war (Article 9), guaranteed extensive human rights including gender equality, and reduced the emperor to a mere symbol. These weren't just legal changes but a revolution in the basis of Japanese political life.

Kojima's 'The American School' (1954) captures what the constitution cannot: the psychological reality of defeat. Japanese English teachers walk four miles on asphalt roads built for American cars to visit a school their taxes paid for but cannot send their children to. The protagonist Isa refuses to speak English as a form of silent resistance, while Yamada performs grotesque deference while secretly boasting of wartime atrocities. The occupation was both democratizing and humiliating - and Japanese had to live with both truths simultaneously.

keywords.definition: Constitutional clause in which Japan 'forever renounce[s] war as a sovereign right' and pledges never to maintain 'land, sea, and air forces'
keywords.relevance: Unique feature defining postwar Japan; debates about revision continue today

keywords.definition: Supreme Commander for Allied Powers - MacArthur's occupation authority that governed Japan 1945-1952
keywords.relevance: Implemented radical reforms including the constitution, land reform, zaibatsu dissolution

keywords.definition: Principle that political power derives from the people rather than the emperor
keywords.relevance: Revolutionary shift from Meiji Constitution where emperor held sovereignty

keywords.definition: Shift in occupation policy (1948-49) toward anti-communism, economic recovery, and limited Japanese rearmament
keywords.relevance: Cold War changed American priorities; democratization took backseat to containing communism

keywords.definition: Article 9's prohibition on war as an instrument of national policy and maintenance of military forces
keywords.relevance: Reinterpreted to allow Self-Defense Forces; source of ongoing constitutional debate

keyPassages.quote: Aspiring sincerely to an international peace based on justice and order, the Japanese people forever renounce war as a sovereign right of the nation.
The Emperor shall be the symbol of the State and of the unity of the people, deriving his position from the will of the people with whom resides sovereign power.
It is foolish for Japanese to speak this language like foreigners. If they do, it makes them foreigners, too. And that is a real disgrace.
The only lesson we'll leave with is the one we've learned just getting here: we lost!

people: Douglas MacArthur - Supreme Commander overseeing occupation and constitution
Emperor Hirohito - retained as symbol but stripped of political power
Beate Sirota Gordon - 22-year-old American who drafted women's rights articles
places: GHQ (General Headquarters) - MacArthur's Tokyo base; center of occupation power
American dependents' schools - symbols of occupation's separate, superior world
Okinawa - remained under US control until 1972; major bases still today
things: 1947 Constitution - new democratic framework drafted in one week by Americans
Article 9 - war renunciation clause unique in world constitutions
Self-Defense Forces - Japan's military by another name, created 1954

keyQuestions.question: Was the occupation democratization or colonization - or both?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Americans imposed reforms but those reforms were genuinely democratic. Japanese had no choice - but many welcomed changes (especially women, labor). The constitution was American-drafted but Japanese-ratified by elected Diet. Isa's refusal to speak English and the teachers' humiliation suggest the psychological cost. Perhaps the question itself is too binary.

keyQuestions.question: Why does Isa refuse to speak English, and what does his resistance represent?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Isa believes speaking English 'like a foreigner' makes you a foreigner - a loss of identity. His refusal is not incompetence but principled resistance. Yet this resistance is also self-defeating - he is an English teacher who cannot teach. Contrast with Yamada's grotesque mimicry. Michiko observes: 'when you spoke it you stopped being yourself.'

keyQuestions.question: What does Yamada's character reveal about postwar Japanese psychology?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Yamada simultaneously boasts of beheading American POWs ('about twenty, I guess') and desperately seeks American approval. He wears leather shoes while colleagues wear army boots. He performs exaggerated deference while harboring contempt. He represents accommodation without genuine transformation - the war criminal rebranded as English teacher.

timeline.event: Japan surrenders; Emperor announces defeat by radio
MacArthur's staff drafts constitution in one week
San Francisco Peace Treaty: occupation ends, Japan sovereign
Kojima publishes 'The American School'; Self-Defense Forces created

connections.fromPrevious: Plate 21 examined Civil War and Revolution in China. While China underwent Maoist revolution, Japan underwent American-supervised transformation. Two very different paths from mid-century crisis.
connections.toNext: Plate 23 examines Mao and Beyond Mao - another attempt to rebuild a society from the ground up, but through revolution rather than occupation. What are the costs and benefits of each path?
connections.broaderThemes: The occupation raises fundamental questions about modernity and democracy: Can democracy be imposed from outside? Is transformation without humiliation possible? How do defeated nations reconstruct identity? Japan's experience contrasts with Germany's more thorough reckoning with the past.

nextPreview.preview: While Japan rebuilt under American tutelage, China underwent Maoist revolution: land reform, Hundred Flowers, Great Leap Forward. How did China's revolutionary path compare to Japan's imposed democratization?
readings: Let Flowers of Many Kinds Bloom (Hundred Flowers)

teachingNotes: The constitution is short and readable - consider having students identify the most surprising provisions
'The American School' is a literary gem - captures complex emotions better than analytical texts; read key passages aloud
The Yamada-Isa contrast is central: accommodation vs. resistance, both psychologically costly
Ask students: Have you ever felt you had to perform a different identity in a foreign language?
The four-mile march is a powerful image - Japanese pedestrians on roads built for American cars
Article 9 debates continue today - consider having students discuss whether Japan should revise it
Yamada's casual mention of beheading POWs is chilling - connects to unresolved war responsibility

locations.name: Hiroshima
locations.description: Atomic bomb, August 6, 1945; symbol of peace movement

locations.name: Nagasaki
locations.description: Atomic bomb, August 9, 1945; second and final nuclear attack

locations.name: Okinawa
locations.description: Remained under US control until 1972; major US bases still
